meta.description: Offline SPL2 reference. Syntax, operators, types, time functions, and examples.

datasets.description: A dataset is a collection of data to search or containing search results. Every SPL2 search references one.

dataset_kinds.description: Time-series event index (tsidx). Supports filtering/aggregation via WHERE or stats commands.
Metrics index (msidx) for metric data. Supports some inline aggregation.
Reference data, typically stored in a KV Store lookup.
Reusable exported SPL2 search. Acts as a virtual dataset.
Materialized results of a search run, identified by a search ID (SID).
Local alias for a dataset from another module.
Target for pipeline output (S3, Index, HEC, devnull, etc.).

datasets.permanent_datasets: Stored on disk (indexes, lookups, views). Each must have a unique name within a module.

temporary_datasets.description: Unsaved, standalone SPL2 datasets. Must be enclosed in square brackets [ ].

datasets.dataset_permissions: Permissions are checked at search runtime. A dataset defined in the same module may still be inaccessible if the user lacks permission.

dataset_literals.description: Inline datasets typed directly into a search, not stored anywhere. Max 30,000 characters.
dataset_literals.format: A JSON-like array of objects. Each object = one event/row.
format_rules: Enclose entire literal in square brackets [ ]
Enclose each object in curly brackets { }
Separate field from value with colon :
Field names with chars other than a-z A-Z 0-9 _ must be in single quotes
String values must be in double quotes

behaviors.with_from: Each object becomes a separate event
behaviors.with_eval: The entire array becomes one event (as a field value)
behaviors.with_union: Events from literal are merged with other datasets

dataset_literals.empty_literal: FROM [{}]  → creates 1 event with only _time field
examples: union customers, orders, [{ vendor: "Acme", city: "NYC", vendorID: 1234 }]

sample_dataset_templates.failed_logins: $events = FROM [{_raw:"Wed Feb 14 2023 23:16:57 mailsv1 sshd[4590]: Failed password for apache from 78.111.167.117 port 3801 ssh2"}]

dataset_functions.description: Functions that create events to form a dataset. Use with any generating command (from, join, union).

functions.name: repeat
functions.description: Create events in a temporary dataset. Similar to makeresults in SPL.

types_of_expressions.description: Expressions produce a value. Used with eval, where, SELECT/WHERE clauses of from, GROUP BY, etc. NOT valid in the search command (which has its own syntax rules).

string_literal.notes: Double quotes required. Backslash IS processed as escape character.

raw_string_literal.notes: Backslash NOT processed. Prefix with @. Double quotes inside must be doubled.

null_literal.notes: Intentional absence of value. Setting a field to null removes the field.

array_literal.notes: Ordered collection. Values can be mixed types.

object_literal.notes: JSON-like object. Internally stored with double-quoted keys.

search_literal.notes: Backtick-enclosed. AND is implied between terms. Lets you use SPL-style matching in FROM/WHERE.

field_reference.notes: Field names starting with non-letter/non-underscore must be in single quotes. Names with special chars (not a-z A-Z 0-9 _) must be in single quotes. No square brackets in field names.

assignment_expression.notes: Replaces existing field or creates new field.

unary_expression.notes: Space required between sign and argument.

parameter_reference.notes: Placeholder requiring a value at search runtime.

lambda_expression.notes: Passes a function as an argument. See lambda_expressions section.

string_templates.description: Embed expressions inside a string literal using ${expression} syntax for readable formatted strings.
string_templates.resolution: Embedded expressions are replaced by their string representations at evaluation time.
examples: | eval difference = "Diff between ${low} and ${high} is ${abs(high - low)}"

field_templates.description: Generate a field NAME dynamically from a template expression using '${expression}' syntax.
notes: Must be enclosed in single quotes
Field names with special chars inside the template must themselves be in single quotes
Keys without special chars can be unquoted, single-quoted, or double-quoted
Keys with spaces or dashes must be in single or double quotes
Duplicate keys in an object literal return an error

access_expressions.description: Access values inside arrays and objects using dot or bracket notation.
access_expressions.case_sensitivity: Keys and values are case-sensitive in access expressions.
access_expressions.reserved_word_keys: Enclose reserved-word keys in single quotes. Example: boardgames.'group'.cooperative[2]

search_literals_in_expressions.description: Search literals let you use SPL-style keyword matching inside FROM/WHERE and with eval/stats.
use_cases: Use SPL commands not supported natively in SPL2
Use in stats count() to count events matching a term
Use in eval if() as a condition
examples: FROM main WHERE sourcetype="secure" AND `invalid user sshd[5258]`
FROM main WHERE host="www3" | `top limit=20 referer`

lambda_expressions.description: Function literals passed as arguments to other functions. Used primarily with JSON functions (any, all, filter, map, reduce).
lambda_expressions.param_type: Default type is any. Specify with colon: ($a: string)
examples: from worldDataset select map(cities, ($val) -> { $region = "emea_" + $val; return $region; }) as regionCities

predicate_expressions.description: Expressions that evaluate to TRUE or FALSE. Used in WHERE/HAVING clauses, where command, and IS operator checks.

AND.description: Both must be TRUE. Implied between terms in search command.
AND.note: search: OR before AND. from/where: AND before OR.

OR.description: At least one must be TRUE.

NOT.description: Must not be TRUE. Applies only to the next term. Use parentheses for multiple terms.

operator_BETWEEN.not_syntax: expression NOT BETWEEN low AND high

operator_EXISTS.description: TRUE if at least one event in the main search correlates with an event in the subsearch.
requirements: Main dataset must be aliased with AS
Subsearch must have a WHERE clause with a correlation expression
Only = operator supported in correlation expression
LIMIT and OFFSET not supported in subsearch
Only valid in WHERE or HAVING clause of the from command
operator_EXISTS.example: FROM orders AS o1 WHERE EXISTS (SELECT prodno FROM products WHERE prodno=o1.prodno AND availability="in stock") GROUP BY span(_time,3h) SELECT custno, orderno, status

operator_IS.description: Test if a value matches a built-in or custom data type.
examples: | eval result = if(employee_id IS id_number, "id", "not id")

regular_expressions.description: SPL2 uses PCRE (Perl Compatible Regular Expressions). Used with rex command and eval functions: match, mvfind, replace.
regular_expressions.pipe_in_regex: Must enclose regex containing | in quotation marks. Example: ...| regex "expression | with pipe"
backslash_notes: In regex, \ escapes special characters (e.g., \. matches a literal period)
For Windows file paths with double backslash (c:\\temp), you need 4 backslashes in search: c:\\\\temp
SPL2 uses * as wildcard; backslash cannot escape * in search strings
regular_expressions.alternation: Use | inside regex for OR. Enclose in quotes if used in search.

overview.description: Functions accept parameters and return a value. Used with commands like eval, stats, where, timechart.

naming_function_arguments.description: Optionally name arguments when calling functions to improve readability and allow any order.
rules: Argument names separated from values by colon :
Lists of values must be enclosed in square brackets [ ]
Named arguments can appear in any order (if all are named)
Unnamed arguments must come before named arguments
To override only some default values, you must name those arguments
examples: | eval response = if(predicate: code=200, true_value: "OK", false_value: "Error")
FROM main | my_sourcetype field=host sourcetype="webaccess" status=200
invalid_examples: coalesce(values: clientip, ipaddress)  ← list must be in [ ]

custom_eval_functions.description: User-defined functions that return a single value. Used with eval, where, and WHERE/SELECT clauses of from.
notes: Parentheses required even with no parameters
Curly braces required even with no SPL statements
Function names must start with a letter or underscore, no spaces
Parameter names must start with $ followed by a letter or _
Default type for parameters: any. Default return type: any
Cannot reference search statements defined outside the function
usage_examples: | FROM jsondata WHERE method="GET" AND is_error(status_code)
FROM $sample SELECT score, round_if(score) as newscore
FROM $sample SELECT score, round_if(score, 4) as newscore  ← override default

custom_command_functions.description: Functions that behave like commands. Two types: generating (used first in pipeline) and non-generating (used after pipe).
notes: Parentheses and curly braces always required
Non-generating functions must have at least one parameter identifying the source dataset (commonly named $source or $data)
Default return type: dataset (unless function contains a terminating command like into)
To invoke: use function name without $ prefix

dataset_literal_example.description: Generating function using a dataset literal for testing

documentation_comments.description: Javadoc-style comments for custom functions that appear in UIs as hover tooltips.

tags.@example: Followed by description and ```spl ... ``` code block

documentation_comments.example:
/**
 * Returns top-N values from a field.
 * @param $source - The dataset.
 * @param $field - The field to aggregate.
 * @param $limit - Max results to return.
 * @example
 * Return top 5 hosts.
 * ```spl
 * FROM main | top host 5
 * ```
 */
function top($source, $field, $limit: int) { ... }

overview.description: SPL2 is loosely typed by default but supports optional strong typing via data types. Use types to add structure, validation, and handling logic.
when_to_use_strong_typing: Routing/filtering data by type in pipelines

array.description: Ordered collection of values (mixed types allowed).

boolean.description: true or false only. Must be lowercase.

dataset.description: A collection of data (index name or dataset literal).

log_span.description: Logarithm-based span: coefficient followed by 'log' and base.

mv.description: Multivalue, ordered collection where values are separated by newlines.

number.description: Union of double, float, int, long. SPL2 handles precision dynamically.

string.description: Sequence of characters. Must be in double quotes.

time_span.description: Time increment for organizing results. Format: <integer><timescale>

custom_data_types.description: User-defined types derived from built-in or other custom types.

constrained.description: Add a WHERE constraint to an existing type.
examples: type score = int WHERE $value BETWEEN 0 AND 100

structured.description: Define a JSON object with named properties.
structured.syntax: type <name> = { prop1: type1, prop2?: type2, *: type3 }
notes: Append ? to make a property optional
* wildcard allows additional properties of any name
*: string restricts wildcard properties to string type
examples: type person = { firstname: string, surname: string, age: int }
type person_flexible = { firstname: string, surname: string, age?: int }
type person_open_ended = { firstname: string, age: int, * }

subtractive.description: Subtract properties of one structured type from another.

checking_data_against_types.custom_function_check: function type_check($data) { return if(($data IS error_code), "true", "false") }

timestamps.storage_format: All timestamps stored as UNIX time in the _time field (seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC).
timestamps.display: Splunk UIs display _time in human-readable format, but internally it is UNIX time.
timestamps.index_time_field: _indextime, when the event was indexed (vs. _time which is when the event occurred)

gmt_vs_utc.GMT: A time zone used in some European/African countries. Never changes for DST.
gmt_vs_utc.UTC: A time standard (not a time zone). The basis for all time zones. Never changes for DST.

time_modifiers.description: Add to a search to constrain the time range. Use = or != operators only.

earliest.description: Earliest event time (_time field). earliest=1 = UNIX epoch.

latest.description: Latest event time. Defaults to now() if omitted.

now.description: Refers to the current moment in time.

examples: WHERE earliest=1 AND latest=now()  ← all time
time_modifiers.legacy_modifiers_caution: daysago, endtime, hoursago, minutesago, monthsago, etc. still work but may be removed in a future release.

relative_time.description: Time relative to the current moment. Syntax: [+|-]<int><unit>[@<snap_unit>]
relative_time.default_integer: If no integer is specified, 1 is assumed. So 'h' = '1h'.

snap_to.description: @ symbol followed by a time unit rounds DOWN to the start of that unit.

examples.@q or @qtr: Start of most recent quarter (Jan 1, Apr 1, Jul 1, Oct 1)

snap_to.chaining: @d+12h  → start of today + 12 hours = noon today
snap_to.order_of_operations: Left side of @ is applied FIRST, then the snap, then any offset after @.

relative_time.note_24h_vs_1d: 24h and 1d are usually but not always equivalent due to Daylight Saving Time boundaries.

time_spans.description: Used to bucket/group results by time increment. Used with GROUP BY span(), bin, stats, timechart.
time_spans.notes: If no integer specified, 1 is used. Timescale is required.
examples: SELECT count(), host, _time FROM index WHERE sourcetype="webaccess" AND `ERROR` GROUP BY host, span(_time, 5m)
| stats count(action) AS count BY _time span=30mins

time_variables.description: Format tokens used with strftime(), strptime(), relative_time(), and now() functions.

date_and_time.description: Date and time in current locale format (e.g., Thu Jul 18 09:30:00 2022)
Date and time with timezone in current locale format

date_only.description: Date in current locale format (e.g., 7/13/2022 for US English)

time_zones.storage: All timestamps stored as UNIX time regardless of timezone.
time_zones.indexer_assumption: The Splunk indexer assumes incoming timestamps use its own timezone unless offset is specified.
timestamp_formats_at_index: With GMT offset: Friday, April 13, 2020 11:45:30 AM GMT -07:00
Local time with no timezone: 10:55AM, assumed same TZ as indexer
time_zones.consistency_note: Searches using absolute UNIX time (e.g., earliest=-24h) return the same events regardless of user timezone.
timezone_divergence_cases: Since 00:00:00 (midnight of user's TZ), different results per timezone
Snap-to times like @d or @mon, different results per timezone
time_zones.best_practice: When exact timezone consistency is required, specify time based on the timezone where the Splunk indexer resides.

lexicographical_order.description: Default sort order used by ORDER BY, sort, search, and timechart commands. Based on UTF-8 / ASCII encoding.
rules: Numbers are sorted BEFORE letters, based on the first digit (not numeric value)
Uppercase letters are sorted BEFORE lowercase letters
Symbols vary, some sort before numbers, others before or after letters
lexicographical_order.number_sort_warning: Numbers are sorted lexicographically (by first digit), NOT numerically. Example: 9 sorts after 100 because '9' > '1'.

case_example.note: Uppercase IDs sort before lowercase IDs

lexicographical_order.custom_sort: You can override lexicographical order with custom sort logic using the sort command.

wildcards.description: Match partial string values. Which wildcard to use depends on the command.

wildcard_by_context.wildcard: LIKE function with % (multiple chars) or _ (single char)
wildcard_by_context.example: WHERE like(ipaddress, "198.%")  or  WHERE name LIKE "Pete_"

wildcard_by_context.wildcard: LIKE function with % (multiple chars) or _ (single char)

wildcard_by_context.context: All other commands (search, SELECT field names, etc.)
wildcard_by_context.example: search sourcetype="access_*"  or  SELECT 'bytes*' FROM main

wildcards.quoting_requirement: Wildcard strings must be enclosed in quotation marks.
best_practices: Put wildcards at the END of a term for best performance
Use field-value pairs when possible: status="fail*" not just fail*
Avoid wildcards in the MIDDLE of strings, causes inconsistent results with punctuation
Avoid wildcards at the BEGINNING (prefix wildcards), causes major performance issues
Avoid wildcard-only searches (* with no other criteria), retrieves everything

wildcards_in_middle_warning.problem: productID="S*G01" fails because terms like SC-MG-G01 are segmented into SC, MG, G01. No single segment starts with S and ends with G01.
wildcards_in_middle_warning.solution: Specify exact IDs or use a lookup table for large value sets

wildcards.wildcards_with_punctuation: Avoid using wildcards to skip over punctuation characters (/, ., -). Specify each string explicitly.
wildcards.searching_for_asterisk: Cannot search for * directly. Search for the surrounding term first, then: | where _raw="\*78" to narrow results.

single_quotes.use_for: Field names that contain special characters, spaces, dashes, wildcards, or numbers as the first character
examples: 'Avg Usage'  ← space in field name (via AS alias)

double_quotes.flexible_syntax: Because values require double quotes, field=value and "value"=field are both valid (except search command, which requires field on left).

backticks.use_for: Search literals, one or more terms to match in event data. AND implied between terms.

field_name_rules_summary: Must start with a-z, A-Z, or _ to avoid single quotes
Any other starting character → single quotes required
Any non-alphanumeric/non-underscore character in name → single quotes required

alternatives: Search literals (`...`), no need to escape quoted strings inside
Raw string literals (@"..."), backslash not processed; only escape needed is "" for double quotes

must_escape.notes: Double quote inside a string value

string_values: Single quotes inside strings do NOT need escaping
Double quotes inside strings MUST be escaped: \"
field_names: Single quote inside a field name MUST be escaped: \'
Example: | eval 'Berlin\'s values' = if(city="Berlin", round(sales), null)
search_literals: Backtick inside a search literal MUST be escaped: \`

json_with_quoted_values.example: WHERE 'quote' LIKE "%\"To be%"  ← searches for JSON text that starts with \"To be

event_segmentation.overview: When data is indexed, the indexer segments (tokenizes) event text. Segments affect how keywords and wildcards match at search time.

major_breakers.description: Divide event text into large tokens

minor_breakers.description: Further divide major-breaker tokens into smaller tokens

search_implications: To search for an IP address, enclose in quotes: WHERE clientip="91.205.189.15"
Wildcards in the middle of punctuated strings fail because segments don't span punctuation
productID="S*G01" fails, no segment starts with S and ends with G01
Avoid wildcards in the middle or beginning of terms
To match punctuation, specify it explicitly rather than using wildcards
best_practices: Enclose values containing punctuation in double quotes
Avoid wildcards in the middle of terms
Avoid prefix wildcards (performance and consistency issues)
Prefer exact field-value pairs over raw keyword searches

comments.overview: SPL2 supports two comment styles: block comments and line comments. Comments are ignored during search execution.

block_comments.description: Can span multiple lines. Can appear inline within a search pipeline.
block_comments.limitation: Block comments cannot be used in any portion of the search that uses the search command.
examples: | eval bytes = k * 1024 /* k contains kbytes */ | stats sum(bytes) BY host

/* This is a multi-line comment.
   Explains the whole search. */
| FROM index=mydataset WHERE source=usgs
| eval Description=case(depth<=70, "Shallow", depth>70 AND depth<=300, "Mid", depth>300, "Deep")
| stats count(), min(mag), max(mag) BY Description

line_comments.description: Comment from // to end of line. Can be placed within any SPL2 command.
examples:
| eval bytes = k * 1024 // the k field contains kilobytes
| stats sum(bytes) by host

| stats count() by $field // COUNTS EVENTS, ORGANIZED BY $FIELD
| sort -count           // SORTS IN DESCENDING ORDER
| head $limit           // RETURNS TOP N RESULTS

documentation_comments.description: Special block comment for custom functions. Appears as hover documentation in UIs.

tags.@example: Followed by description and ```spl code block ```

documentation_comments.placement: Must appear immediately before the function definition.

troubleshooting_use_case.description: Comment out parts of a pipeline to isolate problems.
troubleshooting_use_case.example:
| FROM main WHERE ipaddress LIKE "198.%" // | stats sum(bytes) BY hosts
// Commented out stats to verify field name is 'host' not 'hosts'
